data.title: Punctuation factor
data.statement:
You work for a text-based social app, and in order to drive more engagement, your company wants to implement message scoring, so messages on the home page can be more relevant to the users.

One of the factors used for scoring messages involves checking the punctuation in all messages. It reasons on groups of punctuations, i.e. groups of consecutive {{.}}, {{!}} and {{?}} characters in any order and repetition, such as {{.}}, {{!!?}} or {{.!?..!!?}}.

The score of a punctuation group can be computed by adding the value of every punctuation sign in the group, where:

* {{.}} is valued at 2 points.
* {{!}} is valued at 5 points.
* {{?}} is valued at 10 points.

For example, the score of the group {{!!?}} would be {{5+5+10}}.

The total punctuation of a message is the sum of the score of each group, multiplied by the index of the first character in each group (starting from 1). For example, in the following message:

{{a. b... cd!?}}

There are three punctuation groups:

* {{.}}, valued at 2 points, at index 2.
* {{...}}, valued at 6 points ({{2+2+2}}), at index 5.
* {{!?}}, valued at 15 points ({{5+10}}), at index 11.

The total punctuation factor of the message above would be {{2*2+6*5+15*11=199}}.

If no punctuation groups appear in the message, the computed punctuation factor will be 0.

testCases.title: Multiple single-char punctuation groups
testCases.testIn: I like to write multiple sentences. Don't you? Come on!

testCases.title: Multiple multi-char punctuation groups
testCases.testIn: What the heck?! Is it only possible... to be such a badass????? wtf

testCases.title: Multiple multi-char punctuation groups
testCases.testIn: It must be!!! Or not??? I am not sure... anymore

testCases.title: No punctuation
testCases.testIn: s0 3dgy i don1 us3 punctu4ti0n, exc3pt comm4s

testCases.title: No punctuation
testCases.testIn: just some other things, also i have no inspiration for the final test

testCases.title: Multiple mixed length punctuation groups
testCases.testIn: One... two..... three.... tadaaaa!! Oh wait, you weren't ready? Too bad for you :-p

testCases.title: Multiple mixed length punctuation groups
testCases.testIn: Such a perfect surprise!!!!!! Can't believe how flawless that surprise was. Can you tell I have no idea what to write? Writing tests is hard, I think?!

data.constraints:
1 <= length([[message]]) < 256
[[message]] is ASCII encoded.
[[factor]] can be stored in a 16-bit signed integer.
data.inputDescription: <<First line:>> [[message]] to compute the punctuation factor for.

lastVersion.statementHTML:
Goal

You work for a text-based social app, and in order to drive more engagement, your company wants to implement message scoring, so messages on the home page can be more relevant to the users.

One of the factors used for scoring messages involves checking the punctuation in all messages. It reasons on groups of punctuations, i.e. groups of consecutive ., ! and ? characters in any order and repetition, such as ., !!? or .!?..!!?.

The score of a punctuation group can be computed by adding the value of every punctuation sign in the group, where:

* . is valued at 2 points.
* ! is valued at 5 points.
* ? is valued at 10 points.

For example, the score of the group !!? would be 5+5+10.

The total punctuation of a message is the sum of the score of each group, multiplied by the index of the first character in each group (starting from 1). For example, in the following message:

a. b... cd!?

There are three punctuation groups:

* ., valued at 2 points, at index 2.
* ..., valued at 6 points (2+2+2), at index 5.
* !?, valued at 15 points (5+10), at index 11.

The total punctuation factor of the message above would be 2*2+6*5+15*11=199.

If no punctuation groups appear in the message, the computed punctuation factor will be 0.
Input
First line: message to compute the punctuation factor for.
Output
First line: Computed factor.
Constraints
1 <= length(message) < 256
message is ASCII encoded.
factor can be stored in a 16-bit signed integer.
Example
Input
Hello world!
Output
60
